title: 5.4: The Other Trigonometric Functions

definitions.text: Definition: Tangent, Secant, Cosecant, and Cotangent Functions For the point (\(x\), \(y\)) on a circle of radius \(r\) at an angle of \(\theta\), we can define four addit ional important functions as the ratios of the sides of the corresponding triangle: The tangent function: \[\tan (\theta )=\dfrac{y}{x}\] The secant function: \[\sec (\theta )=\dfrac{r}{x}\] The cosecant function: \[\csc (\theta )=\dfrac{r}{y}\] The cotangent function: \[\cot (\theta )=\dfrac{x}{y}\]
Definition: Tangent, Secant, Cosecant, and Cotangent Functions

examples.text: Example \(\PageIndex{1}\) Evaluate \(\tan (45{}^\circ )\) and \(\sec \left(\dfrac{5\pi }{6} \right)\). Solution Since we know the sine and cosine values for these angles, it makes sense to relate the tangent and secant values back to the sine and cosine values. \[\text{tan} (45^{\circ}) = \dfrac{\text{sin} (45^{\circ})}{\text{cos} (45^{\circ})} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}/2}{\sqrt{2}/2} = 1\nonumber\] Notice this result is consistent with our interpretation of the tangent value as the slope of the line passing through the origin at the given angle: a line at 45 degrees would indeed have a slope of 1. \[\text{sec}(\dfrac{5\pi}{6}) = \dfrac{1}{cos(\dfrac{5\pi}{6})} = \dfrac{1}{-\sqrt{3}/2} = \dfrac{-2}{\sqrt{3}}\quad\text{ ,which could also be written as}\quad \dfrac{-2\sqrt{3}}{3}\nonumber\]
